risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Two hundred ten children, ages 4–11 years old, along with their 164 parents were randomly assigned to one of four treatment conditions..." and "Following the initial assessment, each child was randomly assigned to one of the four treatment conditions." Comment: The paper clearly states that participants were randomly assigned, but it does not describe how the random sequence was generated (e.g. computer-generated list, random number table, blocks, stratification, central randomization). There is no evidence of a non-random method, so High risk is not justified; however, 'randomly assigned' alone without further detail is insufficient for Low risk.

Quote: "Following the initial assessment, each child was randomly assigned to one of the four treatment conditions... Because the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment, participants were told of the treatment assignment by the therapist during the first treatment session." Comment: This indicates the coordinator who did assessments was blinded to assignment at least when informing participants, but the report does not explicitly describe the concealment mechanism used before enrollment/allocation (e.g., central randomization, SNOSE, pharmacy-controlled system). The statement that children were 'randomly assigned' addresses sequence generation, not concealment. Therefore, whether the person enrolling participants could foresee the next assignment is not clearly reported.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Because the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment, participants were told of the treatment assignment by the therapist during the first treatment session." Quote: "TF-CBT, with all the components including the TN component, was only provided to those participants randomly assigned to the Yes TN conditions." Comment: This indicates participants and treating therapists/personnel knew the assigned intervention after randomization and during treatment. The study outcomes were largely subjective and plausibly influenced by such knowledge (e.g. child and parent self-report measures including CDI, Fear Thermometer, MASC, Shame Questionnaire, BDI-II, PERQ, PPQ, and parent-rated CBCL/CSBI). Therefore lack of blinding could affect outcomes, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "Because the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment, participants were told of the treatment assignment by the therapist during the first treatment session." Quote: "Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II) is a 21-item self-report instrument..." "Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) is a 120-item parental rating scale..." "The Children’s Depression Inventory (CDI) is a 27-item self-report instrument..." "Fear Thermometer... helps children rate their sexual abuse-related level of fear/discomfort." Comment: Outcomes were assessed largely by patient/parent self-report and interviewer-administered K-SADS, and participants/parents were explicitly aware of treatment assignment after randomization. These outcomes include multiple subjective measures (child self-report fear/anxiety/depression/shame; parent-rated child behavior; parent self-report distress/parenting), so knowledge of allocation could influence measurement. Although the project coordinator was blind to assignment, this does not protect the self-reported and parent-reported outcomes; under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded subjective outcomes makes the domain high risk.

Quote: "Two hundred ten children, ages 4–11 years old, along with their 164 parents were randomly assigned to one of four treatment conditions... As Figure 1 displays, 16 (8%) children and their parents never returned after being assigned to treatment, and 15 (7%) children and their parents left after attending only one or two sessions. These 31 (15%) children and their parents were defined as dropouts... However, it should be noted that we did run analyses in which all of the patients volunteering for the study were included (intent-to-treat) and compared the results from these analyses with those in which only patients with 3 or more completed treatment sessions were included. There were no statistical differences." Quote: "None of these characteristics or outcome measures was significantly differentiated by... dropping out of treatment, completing post-treatment evaluations..." Quote: "A MM-ANCOVA is robust with respect to the occurrence of randomly missing data. Consequently, all of the available data at each evaluation were employed in the mixed model analyses... A modified intent-to-treat approach was also used... SAS Multiple Imputation... All of the parameter estimates... were similar to those that had been found without imputing missing data." Comment: Post-randomization attrition is reported numerically (31/210, 15%), which is below the usual high-risk threshold and not reported as imbalanced across treatment groups; authors state dropout rates were comparable across the four groups. Missing outcome data were additionally addressed with mixed models using all available data and sensitivity analyses with multiple imputation/modified ITT showing similar results. Although the main presented analyses were restricted to completers with >=3 sessions, the reported attrition was moderate and robustness analyses did not indicate bias.
